skill.name: data-quality-frameworks
skill.description: Implement data quality validation with Great Expectations, dbt tests, and data contracts. Use when building data quality pipelines, implementing validation rules, or establishing data contracts.
skill.summary: Build data quality checks, dbt tests, Great Expectations suites, and data contracts for production pipelines.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The high static score is driven by markdown examples, inline framework names, and documentation links rather than executable skill code. No malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or hidden automation was found. Minor risk remains because the playbook includes user-run setup commands and notification examples that may expose validation details if configured poorly.

low_findings.title: External Command Examples Are Documentation Only
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged shell-like content, but the reviewed lines are markdown examples for installing and initializing Great Expectations or opening the implementation playbook. These examples are not executed by the skill and do not contain dynamic command construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content appears inside markdown instructions and fenced examples. I found no executable script, hook, or user-input command injection path.

low_findings.title: Framework Syntax Misclassified as Security Indicators
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance flags map to ordinary data modeling terms such as primary key, foreign key, UUID format, timestamps, and dbt ref syntax. These are normal data quality concepts and not evidence of malware or system probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context is schema and test documentation, not cryptographic or reconnaissance code. The terms are semantically tied to data contracts and dbt examples.

low_findings.title: Notification Example Could Expose Validation Details
low_findings.description: The Great Expectations checkpoint example includes a Slack webhook placeholder for failure notifications. This is a legitimate pattern, but users should avoid sending sensitive row values or PII in validation alerts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The notification pattern is explicit, but it uses an environment placeholder and does not hardcode a destination. Exposure depends on user configuration and validation result contents.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Are Benign Network References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are links to official documentation for Great Expectations, dbt, data contracts, and Soda Core. They are not fetched by code and do not transmit local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in a markdown Resources section. I found no code path that requests these URLs or sends data to them.

content.value_statement: Data pipelines often fail because teams lack consistent validation rules and clear ownership. This skill helps create Great Expectations suites, dbt tests, and data contracts for repeatable quality checks.
actual_capabilities: Designs Great Expectations suites for schema, uniqueness, validity, freshness, and statistical checks.
Creates dbt schema tests, singular tests, and reusable generic tests for warehouse models.
Drafts data contracts with owners, schema rules, PII labels, quality checks, and service expectations.
Plans automated quality pipelines that run validations and fail builds on critical issues.
Produces practical checklists for monitoring, alerting, documentation, and remediation ownership.
limitations: It does not connect to databases or inspect live datasets by itself.
Generated thresholds need review against real data distributions and business tolerances.
Examples require compatible project setup for Great Expectations, dbt, or contract tooling.
It cannot replace governance approval for PII handling or regulated datasets.

use_cases.title: Add Validation to a New Pipeline
use_cases.description: Define checks for source freshness, schema stability, primary keys, accepted values, and row counts before launch.

use_cases.title: Standardize Analytics Model Tests
use_cases.description: Convert known business rules into dbt tests that run in development, pull requests, and scheduled jobs.

use_cases.title: Create Producer-Consumer Contracts
use_cases.description: Document required fields, ownership, PII classification, quality rules, and freshness commitments between teams.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Quality Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a data quality checklist for my dataset. Include completeness, uniqueness, validity, consistency, freshness, and ownership checks.

prompt_templates.title: Generate dbt Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Design dbt tests for these models and columns: [paste model details]. Include schema tests, relationships, accepted values, and custom business rules.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Data Contract
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a data contract for [dataset name]. Include owner, consumers, schema, PII labels, quality checks, freshness SLA, and change management rules.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an Automated Quality Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an automated data quality pipeline using Great Expectations and dbt for [pipeline]. Include CI checks, scheduled validation, alerting, severity levels, and remediation steps.

output_examples.input: A team needs quality checks for an orders table.
output_examples.output: A structured validation plan covering required order IDs, unique keys, valid statuses, positive amounts, recent timestamps, row count ranges, and owner escalation.

output_examples.input: An analytics team wants tests for customer and order models.
output_examples.output: A dbt testing outline with not-null checks, uniqueness rules, relationships between models, accepted values, freshness checks, and custom business logic tests.

output_examples.input: A platform team needs a contract for a shared event feed.
output_examples.output: A contract summary with producer and consumer owners, required fields, PII classification, quality rules, freshness commitments, and versioning expectations.

best_practices: Start with critical tables, primary keys, freshness, and fields used in downstream decisions.
Set thresholds from observed data and review them with business owners before enforcement.
Keep alert messages concise and avoid exposing sensitive row-level values.
anti_patterns: Do not block production pipelines on new checks before defining a fallback path.
Do not copy example thresholds without validating them against real data.
Do not send PII or sensitive samples in validation reports or chat alerts.

faq.question: Does this skill run Great Expectations or dbt for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance and examples that you adapt and run in your own environment.

faq.question: Can it help choose which checks to start with?
faq.answer: Yes. It prioritizes checks by business impact, failure likelihood, and downstream dependency risk.

faq.question: Does it support data contracts?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes contract patterns for schemas, owners, PII labels, quality rules, and service expectations.

faq.question: Can I use it with only dbt?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance includes dbt schema tests, singular tests, and reusable generic tests.

faq.question: How should teams handle sensitive data in alerts?
faq.answer: Alerts should report failed checks and aggregate metrics, not raw sensitive records or personal data.

faq.question: Is this suitable for regulated data?
faq.answer: It can support regulated workflows, but legal, security, and governance teams must approve final rules.
